7: A shadow: the high priest going alone into the Most Holy Place once a year, not without blood which he offers for himself and for the unintentional sins of the people — the restriction, the solitude, the blood requirement, and the annual repetition are all typological features pointing to their antitypes in Christ's ministry. The restriction (one person per year) → fulfilled in the unique high priest whose once-for-all entry opens the way for all. The blood requirement → fulfilled in his own blood. The annual repetition → abolished by the once-for-all offering.
11: A direct revelation: Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and more perfect tent not made with hands, not of this creation — he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption. The four contrasts (greater tent vs. handmade tent; own blood vs. animal blood; once for all vs. annually; eternal redemption vs. temporary purification) define what the incarnation, cross, resurrection, and ascension accomplished. The "appeared" (paragenomenos) marks the historical event of the Incarnation as the beginning of the true high-priestly ministry that culminated in the heavenly entry.
24: A direct revelation: Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf. The "now" is the present reality of the ascended Christ's ministry — he is presently in God's presence, presently appearing before the Father for the community. The community does not approach an empty throne but the presence of their high priest who is simultaneously at the right hand of God and interceding on their behalf. His appearing for them grounds the community's confidence to approach through him.
28: A direct revelation: Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him. The two appearances structure the entire Christological narrative: the first appearance was the self-offering for sin (the cross and heavenly entry); the second appearance will be the consummation-salvation of those who are waiting. Between the two appearances is the present time of high-priestly intercession (vv24-25) and the community's faith-endurance. The parousia is not a new atonement but the completion of the salvation that the first appearing secured.
1: A shadow: the first covenant had regulations for worship and an earthly sanctuary — the entire cultic structure of the Mosaic covenant is the elaborate shadow of the heavenly reality. The "regulations for worship" are the type's rules; the "earthly sanctuary" is the type's location. Both the rules and the location point beyond themselves: the regulations to the perfect priestly service Christ renders; the earthly sanctuary to the heavenly sanctuary where Christ ministers.
2: A shadow: in the outer room were the lampstand and the table with the bread of the Presence — the furnishings of the Holy Place are the type's first level. The lampstand (light in the sanctuary) prefigures Christ as the light of the world (John 8:12) and the community's illumination through him. The showbread (the bread of the Presence, renewed weekly) prefigures Christ as the bread of life (John 6:35, 48) — the permanent presence of nourishment before God in the true sanctuary.
3: A shadow: behind the second curtain was the Most Holy Place — the graduated access structure of the tabernacle, with the most restricted inner room, is the type of the graduated access that Christ's death tore open. The curtain that blocked all but the high priest's once-yearly entry is the same curtain that was torn from top to bottom at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51) — the type's restriction is removed by the antitype's completed sacrifice.
4: A shadow: the Most Holy Place contained the golden altar of incense, the ark of the covenant, the golden jar of manna, Aaron's staff, and the stone tablets — each object in the type points to Christ. The incense altar (the priestly intercession) → fulfilled in Christ's permanent intercession (Hebrews 7:25). The manna (God's wilderness provision) → fulfilled in Christ as the bread of life (John 6:49-51). Aaron's staff that budded (divine vindication of priesthood through new life) → fulfilled in Christ's resurrection as the divine validation of his priesthood. The stone tablets (the covenant law) → fulfilled in the internalized law of the new covenant (Hebrews 8:10).
5: A shadow: above the ark were the cherubim of Glory overshadowing the mercy seat — the mercy seat (kapporet, the place of atonement) where the blood was sprinkled on Yom Kippur is the type fulfilled in Christ as the mercy seat (Romans 3:25: God set forth Christ as a hilastērion — the same word as the LXX uses for the mercy seat). The glory-cherubim overshadowing the place of atonement is the type of the divine glory that overshadows the true mercy seat — Christ's sacrifice — in the heavenly sanctuary.
6: A shadow: the priests entered the outer room regularly to perform their ministry — the daily priestly ministry in the Holy Place is the shadow of Christ's ongoing heavenly ministry. The lampstand-tending (maintaining the light) and incense-burning (intercession) that occupied the priests daily correspond to Christ's permanent heavenly intercession. The regularity of the earthly priestly routine is the shadow of the permanence of Christ's heavenly service — the shadow's repetition anticipated the antitype's permanence.
8: A shadow: the Holy Spirit was indicating through the tabernacle arrangement that the way into the Most Holy Place was not yet open while the first tabernacle was still standing. The tabernacle's restricted access was a Spirit-designed pedagogical statement: the divine presence was both present and inaccessible under the old covenant — a living visual parable of the coming solution. Christ's death opens what the curtain blocked; the Spirit who designed the restriction is the same Spirit who announces the opening.
9: A shadow: this was a parable for the present time, indicating that gifts and sacrifices offered could not perfect the worshiper's conscience. The entire tabernacle is a divinely designed parable — its external rituals point to an internal transformation they could not achieve. The conscience that needed cleansing (the moral-spiritual interior) was the target the type could only gesture toward. Christ's blood achieves what the parable gestured toward: the conscience is cleansed (Hebrews 9:14), not merely covered.
10: A shadow: the regulations about food and drink and various washings — bodily regulations applying until the time of reformation — are the type's external dimension. Everything in the Levitical system that dealt with bodily purity was pointing forward to the spiritual purity that Christ's blood achieves. The "time of reformation" (kairos diorthōseōs) is the Christ-event: the time when God set right what the shadow had gestured toward.
12: A direct revelation: Christ entered the most holy place once for all, not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption. The three contrasts with the Levitical Day of Atonement — once for all vs. annually; own blood vs. animal blood; eternal redemption vs. repeated coverage — define the qualitative difference. His entry was the fulfillment of every Yom Kippur in Israel's history: the event toward which all those annual approaches were pointing.
13: A type: the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of the red heifer sanctify those who are defiled, purifying the flesh. The Levitical purification is genuine but limited: it achieves external, ceremonial cleanness. The type's achievement (genuine external purification) is the shadow of the antitype's greater achievement (genuine internal purification of the conscience). The type's reality establishes the a fortiori argument: if the shadow achieves this much, how much more does the substance achieve?
14: A direct revelation: the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, will purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God. The conscience-purification that the Levitical type could not achieve is what Christ's blood accomplishes. The eternal Spirit's involvement in the self-offering connects the cross to the entire Trinitarian divine action: the Father sends the Son, the Son offers himself, the Spirit enables the offering that the Father accepts. The purified conscience is then free for the worship that was always the goal: serving the living God.
15: A fulfillment: Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from transgressions committed under the first covenant. The death that redeems from under the first covenant's penalty is Christ's death; the eternal inheritance they receive is the sonship-inheritance that the Son shares with all who belong to him (Hebrews 1:2: "heir of all things"). The new covenant mediator does not replace the covenant structure but fulfills it: the death that the covenant required has occurred in the mediator himself.
16: A direct revelation: where a covenant (will) is made, the death of the one making it must be established. The double meaning of diathēkē (covenant/will) enables the argument: Christ's death is both the covenant-inauguration (his blood is "the blood of the covenant," v20) and the will-activation (his death activates the distribution of the promised inheritance). The testator-Messiah is both the one who makes the will and the one whose death activates it — a unique legal situation with no human parallel.
17: A direct revelation: a will takes effect only at death; it has no force while the testator lives. Christ's death is therefore the necessary precondition for the distribution of everything he promised his community — the Holy Spirit (John 16:7: "I will send the Counselor to you"), the forgiveness of sins, the resurrection hope, the eternal inheritance. Everything that belongs to the Son becomes available to those who receive it through his death. The cross is the moment the will becomes operative.
18: A type: even the first covenant was inaugurated with blood. The Sinai blood-sprinkling (Exodus 24:3-8) is the type of the new covenant's inauguration at the Last Supper and the cross. The pattern — covenant-making requires blood — runs through both covenants, establishing the principle: covenants that address the problem of human sin require the death that sin's penalty demands. The type's blood-inauguration anticipates the antitype's blood-inauguration; the Sinai blood points forward to the cross-blood.
19: A type: Moses sprinkled the scroll of the law and all the people with blood, water, scarlet wool, and hyssop. The Sinai blood-sprinkling covered the covenant document and the covenant people — both were consecrated by blood. The type establishes that both the law and the people must be blood-covered to stand before God. In the antitype, Christ's blood covers not just the law's requirements but the people's consciences — the new covenant's blood reaches what the Sinai blood only touched externally.
20: A type: "This is the blood of the covenant that God commanded you to observe" — the Sinai covenant-formula that Jesus reuses at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:28: "This is my blood of the covenant"). The echo is intentional and Christological: Jesus positions his blood as the fulfillment of the Sinai blood-of-the-covenant, the new covenant-inauguration that the Sinai ceremony was pointing toward. The type's formula is quoted by the antitype, claiming correspondence and fulfillment simultaneously.
21: A type: Moses sprinkled the tabernacle and all the vessels of ministry with blood. The comprehensive blood-consecration of the entire Mosaic worship system establishes that everything involved in approaching God required blood. The principle is total: no approach to God without the blood that covers the approach. In the antitype, Christ's blood is the one comprehensive consecration that covers all approaches — every prayer, every act of worship, every approach to the throne of grace comes through the blood that permanently consecrates the way.
22: A type: under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. The type establishes the blood-forgiveness nexus as the structure of the entire approach to God. This principle reaches its ultimate expression in Christ's self-offering: the blood that achieves the final, complete, permanent forgiveness that all the Levitical blood-shedding anticipated. The "almost everything" of the Levitical system becomes the "one thing" of Christ's sacrifice.
23: A type and fulfillment: the copies of the heavenly things required these purifications, but the heavenly things themselves required better sacrifices. The type-purifications were real but partial; the antitype-purification is complete and final. The "better sacrifices" (singular in effect though plural in formulation) is Christ's one self-offering that purifies not just the earthly copies but the heavenly originals — the community's approach to the divine throne is itself purified by the blood that covers it.
25: A direct revelation: Christ did not enter to offer himself again and again, as the high priest enters the Most Holy Place with blood not his own every year. The non-repetition of Christ's sacrifice is one of the most theologically significant facts in Hebrews: the once-for-all character of the cross is the proof of its completeness. Annual repetition was the type's tacit admission that last year's sacrifice was insufficient; the non-repetition of Christ's sacrifice is the declaration that his sacrifice was fully sufficient — nothing needs to be added, nothing repeated.
26: A direct revelation: Christ appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself. The "end of the ages" (synteleia tōn aiōnōn) frames the crucifixion as the eschatological hinge-point of history — the moment when God acted definitively to address what all prior history had been building toward. "Putting away sin" (athetēsis: legal annulment) describes the cross as the definitive legal resolution of the sin-problem that all the Levitical sacrifices had been acknowledging and managing.
27: A theme: it is appointed for humans to die once, and after that comes judgment. The universal human appointment (one death, one judgment) is the framework against which Christ's own single death and eschatological appearing (v28) are placed. Human mortality is not incidental to the argument but the structure within which Christ's representative humanity operates: he dies the one death (in the place of the many), and at his second appearing the judgment that follows death becomes the salvation for those he represents.
